name: Airbus A310

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome and clean fairings - but in an older 1980s style.
identification.family: The A300/A310 is the original Airbus widebody - the world's first twin-engine widebody. Look for an older-style small upturned winglet (not a sharklet, not an A350 scimitar) and a less swept wing than modern Airbus widebodies.
identification.variant: The A310 is a shortened A300 with a smaller wing - about 46.7m, noticeably stubbier than an A330.

keyTell: Twin-engine widebody but visibly older - smaller upturned winglet, shorter and less swept wing than an A330.

name: Airbus A319

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome, four-pane V-bottom cockpit window, clean wing-body fairing.
identification.family: A320 family - single-aisle twin sitting noticeably high on its gear, with full-round engine inlets (not the 737's flat bottom).
identification.variant: The A319 is the SHORT member of the family - about 33.8m, with just one pair of overwing exits and a fuselage that looks visibly stubby relative to the wing. Often only three pairs of doors instead of the A320's four.

keyTell: Short, stubby A320-family fuselage with one pair of overwing exits.

name: Airbus A318

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome, four-pane V-bottom cockpit window.
identification.family: A320 family - single-aisle twin sitting high on its gear with full-round engine inlets (not the 737's flat bottom).
identification.variant: The A318 is the SMALLEST A320-family member - about 31.4m, even stubbier than the A319. Its giveaway is a NOTICEABLY TALLER vertical tail fin than the rest of the family - Airbus added height to keep enough rudder authority on the short fuselage. Only one pair of overwing exits, and the fuselage looks comically short relative to the wing. Rare in passenger service today (BA's London City–JFK service is the iconic remaining operator).

keyTell: The smallest A320 family member - extra-tall vertical fin on a very short fuselage.

name: Airbus A220-300

identification.manufacturer: Originally a Bombardier CSeries before Airbus took over in 2018, but it carries Airbus-style smooth lines: rounded radome, clean wing-to-body fairing.
identification.family: The A220 is its own family - a clean-sheet narrowbody with very large geared turbofans (Pratt & Whitney PW1500G) that look oversized relative to the slim fuselage. Five-abreast seating (2-3) makes the cabin notably narrower than the A320 family's six-abreast.
identification.variant: The A220-300 is the longer of the two variants - about 38.7m, with three pairs of overwing exits arranged in a distinctive triple cluster.

keyTell: Disproportionately large engines slung under a slim fuselage, plus a pointier nose than the A320 family.

name: Airbus A320

identification.manufacturer: Airbus jets have a smoothly rounded radome (nose), a four-pane cockpit window where the lower edges form a soft V, and a clean blended fairing where the wing meets the fuselage.
identification.family: The A320 family is single-aisle with two underwing turbofans. It sits noticeably higher off the ground than the 737 (longer gear) and lacks the 737's pointed nose and dorsal fin extension.
identification.variant: The A320 is the middle length - one pair of overwing exits (vs two on the A321, vs the markedly shorter A319). At 37.6m it's the original/baseline of the family.

keyTell: Single pair of overwing exits, A320-family nose, sits high on its gear.

name: Airbus A321

identification.manufacturer: Airbus jets have a smoothly rounded radome, a four-pane cockpit window where the lower edges form a soft V, and a clean blended fairing where the wing meets the fuselage.
identification.family: The A320 family is single-aisle with two underwing turbofans, sitting higher off the ground than the 737 and lacking the 737's pointed nose.
identification.variant: The A321 is the longest A320-family jet - long enough to need TWO pairs of overwing exits (the A319 and A320 only have one pair). At 44.5m it's noticeably longer than the A320 (37.6m), so the gap between wing and tail looks proportionally large.

keyTell: Two pairs of overwing emergency exits - the dead giveaway for an A321.

name: Airbus A330-300

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome and cockpit window shape - a more rounded, less aggressive nose than Boeing's widebodies.
identification.family: The A330 is a twin-engine widebody. Compared to the A340 (its visual twin) it has only TWO engines instead of four. Compared to the A350 it has more sharply curved wingtips (no scimitar/curved-up tip - the A330 has a small upturned winglet) and a less stretched, less swept wing.
identification.variant: The A330-300 is the longer variant (~63.7m) with four pairs of doors. The A330-200 is shorter with a taller tail (for range balance).

keyTell: Twin-engine widebody with a small upturned winglet - distinguish from the A350 by its older-style winglet (not a curved scimitar).

name: Airbus A340-600

identification.manufacturer: Airbus widebody - visually IDENTICAL to the A330 in fuselage shape, nose, and tail.
identification.family: The A340 is the four-engine sibling of the A330. Same body, same nose, same tail - but FOUR underwing turbofans instead of two. The only Airbus quad still flying alongside the A380.
identification.variant: The A340-600 is the extreme stretch - 75.4m, briefly the longest airliner in the world. It needs an extra four-wheel center main gear leg under the fuselage to spread its weight.

keyTell: Four engines + Airbus widebody nose = A340. The -600 has an extra center main gear leg under the belly.

name: Airbus A350-900

identification.manufacturer: Smooth Airbus nose and clean wing-body fairing. The A350's signature 'panda eye' black cockpit window mask is one of the most recognizable Airbus features.
identification.family: The A350 is a clean-sheet composite widebody twin. Its wingtips curve gracefully upward in a continuous scimitar (no separate winglet) - the easiest tell vs the A330. The cockpit windows have a distinctive dark surround that sweeps back like sunglasses.
identification.variant: The A350-900 is the baseline (~66.8m). The -1000 is a stretch with a six-wheel main bogie instead of four-wheel.

keyTell: Curved scimitar wingtips (no separate winglet) and the black 'panda eye' cockpit window mask.

name: Airbus A380

identification.manufacturer: Unmistakably Airbus - but in a class of its own.
identification.family: The A380 is the world's only full-length double-decker. Two passenger decks run the entire length of the fuselage, giving it a tall, oval cross-section. Four engines, massive wing.
identification.variant: Only one passenger variant produced (the A380-800).

keyTell: Full-length double deck - no other airliner has this. The 747 has only a partial upper deck.

name: Boeing 727-200

identification.manufacturer: Boeing classic-era jet - pointed nose, swept tail.
identification.family: The 727 is a TRIJET with a T-TAIL. Two engines hang from the rear sides of the fuselage; the third is buried in the tail with an S-duct intake mounted ABOVE the fuselage at the base of the fin. No engines under the wing.
identification.variant: The 727-200 is the long stretch - ~46.7m, the most produced variant.

keyTell: Three engines arranged trijet (two side-mounted at the rear, one in the tail above the fuselage). T-tail.

name: Boeing 737-400 (Classic)

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose, angular cockpit window, dorsal fin extension at the base of the tail.
identification.family: 737 hallmarks: sits LOW to the ground with flat-bottom engine inlets. The Classic generation (-300/-400/-500) lacks the modern blended winglets of the NG, and the engines are smaller (CFM56-3) than NG engines.
identification.variant: The 737-400 is the longest Classic - ~36.4m, with one pair of overwing exits.

keyTell: 737 with NO winglets and smaller, more cylindrical engines - Classic generation.

name: Boeing 737-700

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose with the classic 737 silhouette.
identification.family: 737 NG - flat-bottom engine inlets, low to the ground, blended winglets on most.
identification.variant: The 737-700 is the SHORT NG - about 33.6m, with only one pair of overwing exits and a noticeably stubby look compared to the much more common -800.

keyTell: NG winglets and engines, but a short stubby fuselage - basically a compact -800.

name: Boeing 737-800

identification.manufacturer: Boeing jets have a more pointed radome than Airbus, an angular cockpit window with a swept-back vertical pillar, and a tail fin that meets the fuselage with a small dorsal fillet.
identification.family: The 737 sits noticeably LOW to the ground (it predates large turbofans, so the engines were squashed under short legs). Its engines have a flat-bottom inlet to clear the ground - a unique 737 trait. NG (Next Generation) variants have blended winglets that angle up at ~45°.
identification.variant: The 737-800 is the most common NG variant - about 39.5m, with one pair of overwing exits and a single forward and rear door per side.

keyTell: Flat-bottom engine nacelles that look 'squashed' against the ground, plus blended winglets on most -800s.

name: Boeing 737 MAX 8

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose and angular cockpit window.
identification.family: Still a 737 - sits low to the ground with flat-bottom engine inlets. The MAX has BIGGER engines than the NG (LEAP-1B), pushed forward and slightly up to clear the ground.
identification.variant: The MAX 8's giveaway is the split-tip Advanced Technology winglets - a forked / dual-tip winglet that splits up AND down. It also has a small chined extension at the tail cone (the EE-bay vent), and the engines look noticeably larger than NG engines.

keyTell: Split-tip 'AT' winglets that fork up and down - the easiest way to tell a MAX from an NG.

name: Boeing 747

identification.manufacturer: Boeing widebody - pointed nose, swept tail.
identification.family: The 747 is the only airliner with a HUMP - a partial upper deck stretching from the cockpit roughly a third of the way down the fuselage. Four engines, distinctive wing-to-fuselage anhedral. The 747-8 has a longer hump and raked wingtips; older variants (-400) have winglets.
identification.variant: Major passenger variants: 747-400 (winglets, classic hump length), 747-8 (longer hump, raked wingtips, no winglets).

keyTell: Partial upper-deck hump behind the cockpit - no other airliner has it.

name: Boeing 757

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose. Tail fin meets the fuselage with a tall, narrow profile.
identification.family: The 757 looks like a stretched 737 but ISN'T - it sits MUCH higher off the ground (full-size landing gear), has round engine inlets (no flat bottom), and a distinctive long, narrow fuselage. The wing is more swept and the engines are mounted higher.
identification.variant: The 757-200 is by far the most common (~47.3m); the -300 is a rare longer stretch (~54m).

keyTell: Looks like a 'stretched 737 on stilts' - round engine inlets and tall landing gear separate it from any 737.

name: Boeing 767-300ER

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose, angular cockpit window - a more squared-off look than the 777 or 787.
identification.family: The 767 is a TWIN-engine widebody - but a smaller one than the 777. Engines are noticeably smaller than 777 engines, the fuselage is narrower (7-abreast 2-3-2 instead of 9 or 10 across), and the wing is less swept. Bridges the gap between 757 and 777.
identification.variant: The 767-300ER is the most common variant - ~54.9m, often with winglets retrofitted.

keyTell: Twin-engine widebody, but visibly smaller and slimmer than the 777 - smaller engines and a narrower fuselage.

name: Boeing 777-300ER

identification.manufacturer: Boeing widebody nose and angular cockpit window.
identification.family: The 777 is a twin-engine widebody with the LARGEST turbofans in commercial service (GE90 / GE9X). The engines look enormous - wider than a 737 fuselage cross-section. The wing has a raked wingtip on later variants, with no winglet on earlier ones. A telltale flat-bottom tail cone (the APU section) ends in a cut-off, blade-like profile.
identification.variant: The 777-300ER is the most common long stretch (~73.9m) with raked wingtips and a six-wheel main bogie - very distinctive vs four-wheel widebodies.

keyTell: Massive engines (GE90 family) plus a flat-bladed APU tail cone and six-wheel main gear bogies on the 300ER.

name: Boeing 787 Dreamliner

identification.manufacturer: Boeing nose, but smoother and more rounded than older Boeings (composite fuselage allows the curve).
identification.family: The 787's signature feature is its WING - graceful raked tips that flex visibly upward in flight. The engine nacelles have a chevron (sawtooth) trailing edge for noise reduction, unique to the 787. The cockpit windows have a four-pane layout that wraps further around the nose than the 777's.
identification.variant: The 787-9 is the most common middle-length variant (~62.8m). The -8 is shorter, the -10 is longer.

keyTell: Chevron sawtooth engine nacelle trailing edges, plus extreme wing flex on takeoff.

name: Boeing 717

identification.manufacturer: Boeing inherited the 717 from McDonnell Douglas at the merger - the design is essentially an MD-95.
identification.family: Direct descendant of the DC-9/MD-80 family. T-TAIL with REAR-FUSELAGE engines, but shorter than the MD-80 series and with modern Rolls-Royce BR715 engines that look proportionally large for the small fuselage.
identification.variant: Only one variant produced - about 37.8m.

keyTell: Looks like a stubbier MD-80 with relatively large round engines on the rear fuselage; T-tail.

name: McDonnell Douglas MD-83

identification.manufacturer: McDonnell Douglas inherited the DC-9 family - a long, slender fuselage with rear-mounted engines and a T-tail.
identification.family: The MD-80 series has TWO ENGINES MOUNTED ON THE REAR FUSELAGE (not under the wing) and a T-TAIL (horizontal stabilizer at the top of the vertical fin). Five-abreast seating in a narrow tube, very long and tube-like.
identification.variant: The MD-83 is the most common MD-80 variant - ~45.1m with extended-range fuel.

keyTell: Rear-fuselage engines + T-tail + long slim body. No wing-mounted engines.

name: Embraer E170

identification.manufacturer: Embraer regional jets - slim fuselage with a relatively pointed nose and a sharp angle where the cockpit windows meet the radome.
identification.family: E-Jet - 4-abreast (2-2) seating, underwing engines, conventional tail on a tall fin. The E170/E175 share a fuselage cross-section; the E190/E195 are longer and have a slightly larger cross-section.
identification.variant: The E170 is the SMALLEST E-Jet - ~29.9m. Visually almost identical to the E175 (the E175 is a small stretch of the E170).

keyTell: Smallest E-Jet - extremely short fuselage relative to wing. Easy to confuse with E175.

name: Embraer E175

identification.manufacturer: Embraer regional jet with the slim 4-abreast fuselage and pointed nose.
identification.family: E-Jet - 4-abreast (2-2), underwing engines, conventional tail. E170/E175 share a fuselage cross-section.
identification.variant: The E175 is a small stretch of the E170 - about 31.7m. Often confused with the E190; the E175 is shorter and uses the smaller E170/E175 cross-section. The most common regional jet at US carriers.

keyTell: E-Jet, longer than E170 but shorter than E190 - a small stretch over the E170 with the same nose/tail.

name: Embraer E190

identification.manufacturer: Embraer regional jets have a slim fuselage with a relatively pointed nose and a distinctive sharp angle where the cockpit windows meet the radome.
identification.family: The E-Jet (E170/175/190/195) is a small narrowbody with FOUR-ABREAST seating (2-2, no middle seat). It has a T-tail-ish look but actually a conventional low-mounted tailplane on a tall fin. Engines are slung underwing and look proportionally large vs the narrow fuselage.
identification.variant: The E190 has one pair of overwing exits and a fuselage of ~36.2m - a bigger cross-section than the E170/E175.

keyTell: Slim 4-abreast fuselage with relatively large underwing engines - looks 'pinched' compared to A320/737.

name: Embraer E195-E2

identification.manufacturer: Embraer - same lineage as the E190.
identification.family: E-Jet E2 generation. Unmistakable cue: HUGE Pratt & Whitney geared turbofans (PW1900G), much larger than the original E-Jet engines. Plus a redesigned wing with a CURVED SCIMITAR wingtip (no separate winglet) - the only E-Jet wingtip style like that.
identification.variant: The E195-E2 is the largest E-Jet ever made - ~41.5m.

keyTell: E-Jet shape but with disproportionately large engines and a curved scimitar wingtip - that combo only appears on E2.

name: Bombardier CRJ200

identification.manufacturer: Bombardier CRJs descend from the Challenger business jet - very low-slung fuselage and tiny landing gear.
identification.family: T-TAIL with REAR-MOUNTED engines. Cabin is so narrow there's no real overhead bin clearance - passengers stoop to walk down the aisle.
identification.variant: The CRJ200 is the smallest current CRJ - ~26.8m, ~50 seats, no real overwing exits (just a small emergency hatch). Tiny rectangular engine inlets.

keyTell: Tiny T-tail rear-engine jet with no real overwing exits - the smallest mainline regional jet you'll see.

name: Bombardier CRJ700

identification.manufacturer: Bombardier Canadian regional jet.
identification.family: T-tail, rear-mounted engines, very low to the ground. The 700/900/1000 share a larger fuselage cross-section than the CRJ200 (real overhead bins, ~70+ seats) and a LED light extension at the leading-edge wing root.
identification.variant: The CRJ700 is the shortest of the larger CRJs - ~32.5m. Visibly between the tiny CRJ200 and the longer CRJ900.

keyTell: T-tail rear-engine jet, larger than CRJ200 but visibly shorter than CRJ900.

name: Bombardier CRJ900

identification.manufacturer: Bombardier CRJs descend from the Challenger business jet - very low-slung fuselage, tiny landing gear, and a T-TAIL with engines mounted on the rear fuselage.
identification.family: T-tail (horizontal stabilizer high on the fin), TAIL-MOUNTED engines, extremely low ground clearance - passengers walk up just a few steps. Four-abreast seating.
identification.variant: The CRJ900 has ~36.2m length with one pair of overwing exits - visibly longer than the CRJ700 and much longer than the CRJ200.

keyTell: T-tail with rear-fuselage engines, ground-hugging stance, fuselage length between CRJ700 and the rare CRJ1000.

name: ATR 72

identification.manufacturer: ATR is a Franco-Italian turboprop maker. Their planes look like high-wing turboprops with a flat-bottomed fuselage.
identification.family: Two giveaways: HIGH-MOUNTED WING (mounted on top of the fuselage rather than below) and TURBOPROP engines with six-blade propellers. Conventional tail (not T-tail). Sits very low to the ground, often with airstairs built into the rear door.
identification.variant: The ATR 72 is the longer of the two ATR models (~27.2m). The ATR 42 is shorter with a stubbier look.

keyTell: High wing + propellers + conventional tail - instantly distinguishes it from any jet, and the conventional tail separates it from the Dash 8.

name: De Havilland Dash 8 Q400

identification.manufacturer: Originally de Havilland Canada, then Bombardier, now back to De Havilland - a Canadian high-wing turboprop.
identification.family: Like the ATR, the Dash 8 is a HIGH-WING TURBOPROP. The Q400 distinguishes itself with a T-TAIL (horizontal stabilizer at the top of the fin - ATR is conventional), six-blade props, and a more pointed nose. Faster than ATR (jet-like cruise speed gives it the 'Q' for Quiet branding).
identification.variant: The Q400 is the long stretch (~32.8m); Dash 8-100/200/300 are shorter classic variants.

keyTell: High wing + T-tail + props - that combination uniquely names the Dash 8 Q400.

name: Airbus A300-600

identification.manufacturer: Original Airbus widebody - older-style Airbus radome, less rounded than later jets.
identification.family: The A300/A310 was the world's first twin-engine widebody and Airbus's first product. Older-style small upturned winglet, less swept wing than modern Airbus widebodies.
identification.variant: The A300-600 is the longer, baseline A300 (~54m) - visibly LONGER than the shortened A310. Mostly cargo today: FedEx and UPS are the big remaining operators.

keyTell: Older twin widebody with a small upturned winglet - longer than an A310 but shorter than an A330.

name: Airbus A330-200

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome and small upturned winglet (not scimitar - that's A350).
identification.family: The A330 is a twin-engine widebody. Same nose, wing and engines as the -300; differs only in fuselage length and tail height.
identification.variant: The A330-200 is the SHORTER A330 (~58.8m vs 63.7m for the -300) with a NOTICEABLY TALLER vertical tail - Airbus added fin height to keep enough rudder authority on the shortened airframe. Used heavily for long-thin routes.

keyTell: A330 with a visibly TALLER vertical fin and shorter fuselage than the -300.

name: Airbus A330-900 (neo)

identification.manufacturer: Airbus widebody but with new-generation cues borrowed from the A350.
identification.family: Same A330 fuselage as the ceo, but with NEW raked, A350-style SHARKLET wingtips (curved scimitar tips, not the small upturned winglet of the original A330). Also has bigger Rolls-Royce Trent 7000 engines and a black 'panda-eye' style cockpit window mask similar to the A350.
identification.variant: The A330-900 is the standard neo (~63.7m). Operators: Delta, TAP Portugal, Air Mauritius, Cebu Pacific, Iberojet.

keyTell: Looks like an A330 but with A350-style scimitar wingtips and dark cockpit window mask - that combo only appears on the A330neo.

name: Airbus A350-1000

identification.manufacturer: Airbus composite widebody - scimitar wingtips and 'panda-eye' cockpit mask, same as the -900.
identification.family: A350 family - clean-sheet composite widebody with curved scimitar wingtips and dark cockpit window surround. Twin-engine, six-abreast in business and nine-abreast in economy.
identification.variant: The A350-1000 is the LONG STRETCH (~73.8m vs 66.8m for the -900). Telltale: SIX-WHEEL main landing gear bogies (the -900 has four-wheel) and an extra pair of doors. Operators: Qatar Airways, British Airways, Cathay Pacific, Virgin Atlantic.

keyTell: A350 with SIX-WHEEL main gear bogies - only the -1000 has those.

name: Boeing 737-900ER

identification.manufacturer: Pointed Boeing nose with the classic 737 silhouette.
identification.family: 737 NG - flat-bottom engine inlets, sits low to the ground, blended winglets on most.
identification.variant: The 737-900ER is the LONGEST 737 NG (~42m) - long enough that it needs TWO pairs of overwing exits, just like an A321. That extra exit pair is the easiest tell vs the -800 (one pair). Big operators: Alaska, Korean, Delta, United.

keyTell: 737 NG with TWO pairs of overwing exits - only the -900 has that.

name: Boeing 777-9 (777X)

identification.manufacturer: Boeing widebody nose, evolved 777 silhouette but cleaner.
identification.family: 777X is the next-generation 777. Even larger than the -300ER, with massive GE9X engines (the largest commercial jet engines ever built - wider than a 737 fuselage cross-section).
identification.variant: The 777-9 is the long stretch (~76.7m - longer than any other commercial airliner currently in service). Unique tell: FOLDING WINGTIPS that fold UP at the gate so it can use existing 777 jetway slots. Entering service 2025–26 with Lufthansa as launch customer.

keyTell: Folding wingtips that fold up at the gate, plus the largest commercial engines ever - only the 777X has both.

name: Boeing 777-200ER

identification.manufacturer: Boeing widebody nose and angular cockpit window.
identification.family: 777 family - twin widebody with the largest commercial turbofans (PW4000/Trent 800/GE90).
identification.variant: The 777-200/-200ER is the SHORTER 777 (~63.7m vs 73.9m for the -300ER). Often has either a four-wheel or six-wheel main bogie depending on variant. Big operators: United, ANA, BA, Air France.

keyTell: Standard 777 silhouette but visibly shorter than the -300ER - fewer windows, shorter fuselage.

name: McDonnell Douglas MD-90

identification.manufacturer: Same DC-9/MD-80 lineage - long slim fuselage, T-tail, rear-mounted engines.
identification.family: MD-80 series silhouette: T-tail with horizontal stabilizer at the top of the fin, two engines on the rear fuselage, no wing-mounted engines.
identification.variant: The MD-90 swapped the older squashed-looking JT8D engines for noticeably LARGER and ROUNDER IAE V2500 engines. Slightly longer than the MD-83. Mostly retired (Delta retired theirs in 2020); a few in cargo service today.

keyTell: MD-80-family layout but with much LARGER, rounder V2500 engines on the rear fuselage.

name: Bombardier CRJ-1000

identification.manufacturer: Bombardier Canadian regional jet.
identification.family: T-tail, rear-mounted engines, very low to the ground. Shares the larger 700/900/1000 fuselage cross-section with real overhead bins.
identification.variant: The CRJ-1000 is the LONGEST CRJ - about 39.1m, with TWO pairs of overwing exits (vs one on the CRJ900). Big operators: Air Nostrum (Spain), HOP! / Air France.

keyTell: Longest of the CRJ family - visibly longer than CRJ900, with two pairs of overwing exits.

name: ATR 42

identification.manufacturer: ATR - Franco-Italian high-wing turboprop maker.
identification.family: Same as ATR 72: HIGH-WING with TURBOPROP engines and a CONVENTIONAL tail (not T-tail like the Dash 8). Sits very low to the ground.
identification.variant: The ATR 42 is the SHORTER sibling (~22.7m vs 27.2m for the ATR 72). Very stubby fuselage relative to the wing. Often used for the smallest regional and island routes.

keyTell: ATR 72's twin sister - same shape but distinctly stubbier fuselage.

name: De Havilland Dash 8-300

identification.manufacturer: De Havilland Canada / Bombardier high-wing turboprop.
identification.family: Like the Q400, a HIGH-WING TURBOPROP with T-TAIL. Same overall silhouette.
identification.variant: The Dash 8 Classic (-100/-200/-300) is the SHORTER, OLDER family - about 22–26m vs the Q400's 32.8m. Easiest tell vs the Q400: FOUR-blade props (the Q400 uses six-blade props), and a noticeably shorter fuselage. Still flying with regional carriers.

keyTell: Dash 8 silhouette but shorter than Q400 with FOUR-blade propellers (Q400 has six).

name: Sukhoi Superjet 100

identification.manufacturer: Sukhoi - Russian regional jet maker. Long, pointier nose than Western regionals.
identification.family: Looks broadly like an Embraer E-Jet at a glance: 5-abreast slim fuselage, underwing engines, conventional tail. But the nose is more sharply pointed and the cockpit windows wrap further around the radome.
identification.variant: Single main variant (~30m). Operated almost entirely by Russian carriers (Aeroflot, Rossiya) and a few elsewhere; ~150 active.

keyTell: E-Jet silhouette but with a sharper, more pointed nose and Russian liveries.

name: COMAC C919

identification.manufacturer: COMAC - Chinese state-owned manufacturer. Externally a near-twin of the A320.
identification.family: Looks like an A320: single-aisle twin with under-wing turbofans (CFM LEAP-1C, same family as the A320neo's). Conventional tail, six-abreast cabin.
identification.variant: Single passenger variant (~38.9m). Distinctive sharklets with a slightly different shape than Airbus's. Almost always seen in Chinese airline liveries (China Eastern launch operator, plus Air China, China Southern).

keyTell: A320 lookalike in Chinese airline livery - only obvious from the carrier.

name: Airbus A220-100

identification.manufacturer: Bombardier-designed cockpit (now Airbus), small Airbus-style radome.
identification.family: A220 - narrowbody twin sitting low to the ground with disproportionately large geared turbofan engines for its size.
identification.variant: The A220-100 is the shorter of the two A220s - about 35.0m, with two pairs of overwing exits in a tight cluster (the -300 has three pairs).

keyTell: Short A220 with two overwing exit pairs (vs three on the -300).

name: Airbus A320neo

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome, four-pane V-bottom cockpit window, clean wing-body fairing.
identification.family: A320 family - single-aisle twin sitting noticeably high on its gear, with full-round engine inlets.
identification.variant: The A320neo has noticeably LARGER engines (PW1100G geared turbofan or LEAP-1A) than the CEO and tall scimitar-shaped SHARKLETS at the wingtips (vs the older CEO's small angle-cut wingtip fence).

keyTell: A320 with tall scimitar sharklets and oversized geared turbofan engines.

name: Airbus A321neo

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome, four-pane V-bottom cockpit window.
identification.family: A320 family - longest member with two pairs of overwing exits.
identification.variant: A321neo has the same long fuselage as the CEO (~44.5m) but with sharklets and large LEAP/PW1100G engines. The LR and XLR sub-variants add belly fuel tanks for transatlantic range.

keyTell: Longest A320-family jet with sharklets and oversized engines.

name: Airbus A340-300

identification.manufacturer: Airbus radome and clean fairings, classic 1990s Airbus widebody style.
identification.family: A340 - four-engine Airbus widebody. From a distance very similar to the A330; the four engines are the giveaway.
identification.variant: The A340-300 is the original long-range A340 (~63.7m) - same length as an A330-300 but with FOUR engines and no center main landing gear (the -500 and -600 add a center gear leg).

keyTell: Four-engine Airbus widebody, ~64m long, four-wheel main bogies only (no center gear).

name: Boeing 737 MAX 9

identification.manufacturer: Boeing pointed nose with sharp lower cockpit window edge.
identification.family: 737 MAX - oversized LEAP-1B engines, split-tip Advanced Technology winglets, EE-bay tail vent.
identification.variant: MAX 9 is the longer MAX (~42.2m) - same engines and winglets as the MAX 8 but with TWO pairs of overwing exits (vs one on MAX 8). Operators: Alaska, United, Copa.

keyTell: 737 MAX with two pairs of overwing exits and a longer fuselage than the MAX 8.

name: Boeing 747-8

identification.manufacturer: Boeing pointed nose with the classic 747 hump on top.
identification.family: 747 - four-engine widebody with the partial upper-deck hump on the front of the fuselage.
identification.variant: 747-8 is the modern stretch (~76.3m) with RAKED wingtips (no winglets) and GEnx engines. The hump is noticeably LONGER than the -400 (extending further back along the fuselage). Operators: Lufthansa, Korean, Air China; freighter: UPS, Cargolux, Atlas, Polar.

keyTell: 747 with raked wingtips (NO winglets) and an extended longer hump.

name: Boeing 777-300

identification.manufacturer: Boeing pointed nose, classic 777 fairings.
identification.family: 777 - twin-engine widebody with very large GE90/Trent/PW4000 engines and a six-wheel main bogie.
identification.variant: The non-ER 777-300 is rare today (most retired). Same length as the -300ER (~73.9m) but with smaller PW4090/Trent 892 engines and SQUARED-OFF wingtips (no rake). Last operators were ANA and a handful of carriers.

keyTell: 777 stretch with SQUARED (not raked) wingtips - distinguishes it from the much more common -300ER.

name: Boeing 787-8

identification.manufacturer: Boeing pointed nose, distinctive 787 four-pane cockpit window with deep slope.
identification.family: 787 Dreamliner - twin engines with chevron-edged exhaust nozzles, raked carbon-fiber wings that flex visibly upward in flight.
identification.variant: The 787-8 is the SHORTEST 787 (~56.7m) - visibly stubbier than the -9's 62.8m or the -10's 68.3m. Operators: ANA, JAL, United, Norwegian, Qatar, BA, Air India.

keyTell: Shortest 787 - stubbier silhouette than the -9 or -10.

name: Boeing 787-10

identification.manufacturer: Boeing pointed nose, distinctive 787 four-pane cockpit window with deep slope.
identification.family: 787 Dreamliner - twin engines with chevron-edged exhaust nozzles, raked carbon-fiber wings that flex visibly upward in flight.
identification.variant: The 787-10 is the LONGEST 787 (~68.3m) - noticeably longer than the -9's 62.8m, with the SAME wing and engines as the -9 (no range increase, just extra fuselage). Operators: United, Singapore, Etihad, KLM.

keyTell: Longest 787 - same wing as -9 but stretched fuselage.

name: Embraer E190-E2

identification.manufacturer: Embraer pointed nose, T-tail.
identification.family: E-Jet E2 - second-generation E-Jet with much LARGER Pratt geared turbofan engines (vs the smaller CF34 of the E1) and redesigned raked wingtips.
identification.variant: E190-E2 is the middle E2 (~36.2m) - same fuselage length as the E1 E190 but with the giant geared turbofans and raked tips. Operators: KLM Cityhopper, Helvetic, Air Astana, Aeromexico Connect.

keyTell: E190-sized fuselage with the new oversized geared turbofan engines and raked wingtips.

name: McDonnell Douglas MD-11

identification.manufacturer: McDonnell Douglas style - rounded nose, three-engine widebody silhouette derived from the DC-10.
identification.family: MD-11 - three-engine widebody. The third engine is mounted high on the rear fuselage with an S-duct intake at the base of the tail.
identification.variant: Single passenger variant. Distinctive trijet layout with winglets and swept wings. Almost entirely cargo today: FedEx and UPS run the largest fleets; Lufthansa Cargo retired theirs in 2021. Passenger operators all retired by 2014.

keyTell: Three-engine widebody with the third engine mounted high on the rear fuselage - the only such trijet still flying in significant numbers.
